extra.reasoning:
The property globally(opted_out implies not sent) holds for all reachable executions.

Key analysis of how the two relevant variables can be written:

1. opted_out is monotonic. The only transition that writes opted_out is t1 (on optout), which sets it to true. No transition ever sets opted_out back to false. So once opted_out becomes true it stays true.

2. The only transition that sets sent := true is t2 (on send_attempt), whose guard is `not opted_out`. Therefore sent can only be made true while opted_out is false.

3. t1 (optout) sets opted_out := true AND sent := false in the same atomic update. So the instant opted_out becomes true, sent is simultaneously cleared to false.

4. While opted_out is true: t2 is permanently disabled (guard not opted_out fails, and opted_out never reverts). The other send_attempt transition t21 sets sent := false. All key transitions t3–t20 set sent := false. No enabled transition while opted_out is true can ever set sent to true.

Hence in every reachable state, opted_out=true implies sent=false. sent=true is only reachable while opted_out=false (via t2), and the optout event that flips opted_out to true also zeroes sent. The implication holds at every step of every execution (regardless of length, including beyond 9 events). No counterexample exists.
